_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for New Kent County, VA. Record survival for the corridor county between Williamsburg and Richmond — significant record loss during the Civil War Peninsula Campaign of 1862.
display_name: New Kent County, Virginia — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: New Kent, VA — Route 249 at the courthouse village

previous_courthouses.location: New Kent Court House village — on the main road between Williamsburg and Richmond
previous_courthouses.fate: The courthouse was occupied by Union forces during the Peninsula Campaign in May 1862. McClellan used it as a headquarters. Records were damaged, scattered, or destroyed during the military occupation. Some early records — wills, deeds, court orders — were lost.

record_losses.cause: Civil War — Peninsula Campaign. Union army occupation of New Kent Court House.
records_survived: some deed books from the 18th century onward
record_losses.description: McClellan's Army of the Potomac advanced through New Kent County in May 1862 on the road to Richmond. The courthouse at New Kent Court House was used as a military headquarters and hospital. Records were damaged by military use — some volumes were destroyed, others scattered. The extent of the loss is debated, but the earliest records suffered the most. Some records may have been removed by Confederate officials before the occupation; not all were recovered.
record_losses.narrative_impact: New Kent County's record loss is one of the most significant genealogical casualties of the Civil War in Virginia. For {ancestor_name}'s family, the war did not just disrupt their lives — it may have erased the documentary evidence that they ever lived in the county at all. Research must rely heavily on substitute sources.
record_losses.source: Sears, Stephen W. To the Gates of Richmond. Boston: Ticknor & Fields, 1992.

deeds.completeness: Fragmentary before 1862. Some 18th-century deed books survive but with significant gaps. Post-Civil War deeds are more complete.
deeds.gaps: Major gaps in the earliest records due to Civil War damage. The pre-1700 deed record is essentially lost. 18th-century coverage is partial.
deeds.repository: New Kent County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia, Richmond
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Library of Virginia digital collections; Ancestry.com VA land records (limited)

wills_estates.completeness: Fragmentary. Some 18th-century will books survive, but coverage is uneven. Estate files are scattered.
wills_estates.gaps: Pre-1700 wills are essentially lost. 18th-century coverage has significant gaps.
wills_estates.repository: New Kent County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Library of Virginia digital collections

marriages.completeness: Marriage bonds from approximately 1790. Earlier marriages documented only in church records (St. Peter's Parish).
marriages.gaps: No bonds survive before approximately 1790. St. Peter's Parish records are the primary substitute.
marriages.repository: New Kent County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia

court_minutes.completeness: Fragmentary. Some volumes survive but the series is incomplete.
court_minutes.gaps: Significant gaps throughout. The court record is the most heavily damaged of New Kent's record types.
court_minutes.repository: Library of Virginia; New Kent County Clerk of Court

vital_records.completeness: Virginia state registration began 1912. Earlier vital events must be reconstructed from church records and census data.
vital_records.repository: Virginia Department of Health, Office of Vital Records

church_records.parish: St. Peter's Parish (Church of England / Episcopal)
church_records.completeness: St. Peter's Parish records survive from approximately 1680. The parish register — which recorded the marriage of Martha Dandridge to George Washington in 1759 — is a critically important substitute source given the county's civil record losses.
church_records.repository: Library of Virginia; St. Peter's Episcopal Church, New Kent; published transcriptions

substitute_sources.description: St. Peter's Parish records survive from approximately 1680 and include the parish register (baptisms, marriages, burials), vestry minutes, tithable lists, and processioning records. Given the county's civil record losses, these church records are indispensable.
substitute_sources.repository: Library of Virginia; St. Peter's Episcopal Church; published transcriptions
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: St. Peter's Parish records are the single most important substitute source for New Kent County. They fill gaps left by Civil War record destruction and provide family relationship evidence unavailable elsewhere. The parish register records marriages, baptisms, and burials for the gentry and middling families.
substitute_sources.source: Slaughter, Philip. A History of St. Mark's Parish, Culpeper County, Virginia. Baltimore, 1877. (Parish record methodology reference.)

substitute_sources.description: Land patents from the Crown survive from 1623 at the Library of Virginia. For New Kent families whose county deeds are lost, the patent books document original grants.
substitute_sources.repository: Library of Virginia; published abstracts by Nugent
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Patent books are essential for New Kent County research because they document land grants that the county deed books may no longer contain.
substitute_sources.source: Nugent, Nell Marion. Cavaliers and Pioneers. Richmond: Virginia State Library, 1934.

substitute_sources.description: New Kent was formed from York County in 1654. King and Queen County was formed from New Kent in 1691, and King William from King and Queen in 1702. Cross-referencing neighboring county records can recover family connections lost in New Kent's damaged records.
substitute_sources.repository: Library of Virginia; respective county clerk offices
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Families frequently appeared in records of adjacent counties. York County records (surviving from 1633) may document New Kent families before 1654. King and Queen records may document families who moved from New Kent after 1691.
substitute_sources.source: Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. For a county with significant civil record losses, census records become a critical baseline.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Census records provide the framework for family reconstruction when county records are fragmentary. The 1810 and 1820 censuses are particularly important for bridging gaps in the county record.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, New Kent County, VA.

narrative_hooks: The Civil War destroyed some of New Kent County's oldest records — and if {ancestor_name}'s deed or will was in the courthouse when McClellan's army came through in May 1862, it may be gone. The war did not just change lives in this county — it erased the documentation of lives that had already been lived.

St. Peter's Parish register — the same book that recorded Martha Dandridge's marriage to George Washington in 1759 — may be the best surviving evidence of {ancestor_name}'s existence in New Kent County, because the church records outlasted the courthouse records.

New Kent's record loss means that research must cast a wider net — York County records before 1654, King and Queen records after 1691, colonial patent books, and St. Peter's Parish register may together reconstruct what the courthouse fire could not preserve.

If {ancestor_name}'s record survives in New Kent County despite the Civil War damage, it survived through luck, not design — the volumes that escaped the military occupation were the ones that happened to be in the right place on the wrong day.
source_references: Sears, Stephen W. To the Gates of Richmond: The Peninsula Campaign. Boston: Ticknor & Fields, 1992.
Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.
Nugent, Nell Marion. Cavaliers and Pioneers: Abstracts of Virginia Land Patents and Grants. Richmond: Virginia State Library, 1934.
National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, New Kent County, VA.
